varieties.name: Wild Type (Terpinen-4-ol Chemotype)
varieties.origin: Northern NSW/Southeast Queensland, Australia (traditional Bundjalung lands)
varieties.traits: Small tree to 7m, bushy crown, narrow needle-like leaves 10-35mm, papery white bark
varieties.growing_notes: Natural to swampy coastal Brisbane regions. Fast-growing in subtropical conditions, several feet per season. Excellent heat and humidity tolerance. Used traditionally by Indigenous Bundjalung people for medicine. Primary commercial tea tree oil source. Prefers moist soils near streams.

varieties.name: Revolution Green
varieties.traits: Dense upright shrub/small tree 3-4m tall x 2-3m wide, vibrant needle-like green foliage year-round
varieties.growing_notes: Perfect for Brisbane subtropical conditions. Tolerates heat, humidity, and coastal conditions. Drought-tolerant once established. Suitable for hedging, screens, ornamental gardens. Aromatic foliage when crushed.

varieties.name: Revolution Gold
varieties.traits: Dense upright shrub 4m x 3m, attractive golden foliage (more intense in full sun)
varieties.growing_notes: Excellent for Brisbane climate, thrives in subtropical heat and humidity. Color-contrast screening plant. Tolerates most soil types including clay and sandy loam. Fast-growing, responds well to pruning. Attracts native wildlife.
varieties.availability: Common in Queensland nurseries, garden centers

varieties.name: Golden Gem
varieties.traits: Compact dwarf form with golden foliage, smaller than Revolution Gold

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.origin: Queensland to NSW coast, also New Caledonia and PNG
varieties.traits: Medium-large tree 8-25m, thick white/cream papery bark, weeping branches. Leaves lanceolate to elliptic 40-70mm x 25mm, 5 prominent veins
varieties.yield_notes: High flower production for nectar, traditional bark uses
varieties.growing_notes: Native to coastal Brisbane region (latitude 26°S). Extremely well-adapted to subtropical Queensland. Tolerates waterlogged to dry conditions. Frost-hardy to -5°C. Excellent for poorly drained sites, swampy areas. Bird-attracting. Aboriginal uses: bark for wrapping food, cradles, pillows, bandages.
varieties.availability: Common in Brisbane nurseries as landscape tree

varieties.name: Red-flowered Form
varieties.growing_notes: Same subtropical Brisbane tolerance as standard form. Ornamental value. Limited availability.

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.origin: Northern Australia, extending to Brisbane region
varieties.traits: Large tree to 20m+ with weeping branches, thick white/cream/pinkish papery bark
varieties.growing_notes: Grows well in Brisbane subtropical conditions. Popular as park/street tree. Tolerates poor, waterlogged soils. Long flowering season attracts birds and bees. First Melaleuca described by Linnaeus (1762) from Indonesian specimen.
varieties.availability: Available in Brisbane as landscape specimen

varieties.name: Subspecies cajuputi
varieties.traits: Medium-large tree to 35m (occasionally 46m), grey/brownish/white papery bark
varieties.growing_notes: Subtropical Brisbane climate suitable. Grows in woodland, savannah. Source of cajuput essential oil. Limited ornamental cultivation in Brisbane area.

varieties.name: Subspecies platyphylla
varieties.origin: North Queensland (Cairns region), PNG, Torres Strait
varieties.growing_notes: Native to tropical/subtropical Queensland. Suitable for Brisbane's warmer areas.

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.growing_notes: Suitable for subtropical Brisbane. High antibacterial activity in essential oil. Common in melaleuca forest vegetation type.

varieties.name: Wild Type
varieties.traits: Shrub/small tree 2-5m (occasionally to 15m), prickly needle-like leaves 7-20mm
varieties.growing_notes: Adaptable to Brisbane subtropical conditions. Frost-hardy to -10°C. Prefers well-drained acidic soils. Source of manuka honey. Māori traditional medicine uses.

varieties.name: Ruby Glow
varieties.traits: Compact upright shrub 180-240cm tall x 120-150cm wide, dark green needle-like leaves
varieties.growing_notes: Suitable for Brisbane with protection. Zone 9-11. Aromatic foliage, drought-tolerant established. Excellent containers/borders.

varieties.name: Red Damask
varieties.growing_notes: Dense medium shrub 1.8-2.5m. Suitable for Brisbane subtropical gardens.

varieties.name: Apple Blossom
varieties.growing_notes: 8ft tall and wide (240cm). Brisbane-suitable with good drainage.

varieties.name: Burgundy Queen
varieties.growing_notes: Upright dense shrub to 3.6m tall x 3m wide. Suitable for Brisbane landscapes.

varieties.name: Helene Strybing
varieties.growing_notes: Open upright arching growth to 3m. Brisbane-adaptable.

varieties.name: Red Ensign
varieties.growing_notes: Upright dense shrub 2.4-3m. Excellent Brisbane ornamental.

varieties.name: Snow White
varieties.growing_notes: Clean white display for Brisbane gardens.

varieties.name: Fantasia
varieties.growing_notes: 1.5m tall x 1m wide. Suitable for Brisbane with protection.

varieties.name: Pacific Beauty
varieties.traits: Hardy shrub/small tree to 7m, aromatic foliage
varieties.growing_notes: Native Queensland/NSW coast. Excellent Brisbane subtropical adaptation. Source of jelly bush honey (lemon-scented tea tree honey). Frost-hardy to -8°C. Useful for large arrangements.

varieties.name: Cardwell Form
varieties.growing_notes: Native to tropical/subtropical Queensland. Well-suited to Brisbane climate.

varieties.name: Variety B (Australian Rose)
varieties.traits: Rare small tree, extremely pleasant lemony aromatic foliage
varieties.growing_notes: Brisbane-suitable subtropical plant. Three chemotypes exist. Lemon-scented foliage when crushed. Limited commercial availability.

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.growing_notes: Grows naturally on beach sand. Excellent Brisbane coastal conditions tolerance. Used for erosion control. Frost-hardy to -10°C.

varieties.name: Terpinen-4-ol Chemotype
varieties.origin: Australian, cultivated in India (Kolenchery, Kerala area)
varieties.traits: Small tree to 7m, bushy crown, narrow needle-like leaves, papery bark
varieties.growing_notes: Successfully cultivated in Kerala tropical monsoon climate. Grows well in humid subtropical to tropical areas with 800-1600mm annual rainfall. Prefers frost-free areas (annual lows 9-21°C, highs 22-35°C). Essential oil production established in India. Adapts to Kerala's hot, humid conditions. Requires well-drained moist soils.
varieties.availability: Limited; specialty nurseries, commercial oil plantations

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.origin: Northern Australia, extending to Southeast Asia (Malaysia, Indonesia)
varieties.traits: Large tree to 20m+, weeping branches, thick white/pinkish papery bark
varieties.growing_notes: Known as 'gelam' in Malaysia. Naturally occurs in Southeast Asian swamp forests. Well-adapted to tropical monsoon climates like Kerala. Tolerates waterlogged conditions and seasonal flooding. Source of cajuput-type essential oil. Traditional uses in Malaysia for timber and oil.
varieties.availability: May be available through botanical gardens or specialty imports

varieties.name: Subspecies cajuputi
varieties.traits: Medium-large tree to 35m, white/grey-white papery bark (kayu putih = white wood)
varieties.growing_notes: Native to tropical Asia, including regions with similar climate to Kerala. Grows in coastal swamp forests. Excellent adaptation to tropical monsoon conditions with heavy rainfall. Commercial source of cajuput oil widely used in Southeast Asia. Traditional medicinal uses for infections, respiratory issues.
varieties.availability: Potentially available in Kerala through Asian trade networks or botanical gardens

varieties.name: Subspecies cumingiana
varieties.origin: Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, Malaysia, Indonesia (Sumatra, Java, Kalimantan)
varieties.traits: Leaves 40-200mm long x 10-20mm wide, 7-9 stamens per bundle
varieties.growing_notes: Southeast Asian coastal swamp forest native. Suited to Kerala's tropical monsoon climate. Flowering February-December.

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.origin: New Caledonia, Papua New Guinea, coastal eastern Australia
varieties.traits: Tree 8-25m, thick papery bark, leaves 40-70mm with 5 veins
varieties.growing_notes: Grows in tropical to subtropical climates (similar latitude to Kerala). Tolerates swamps, floodplains, wet soils. Vigorously resprouts after fire. Cultivated as ornamental in tropical regions worldwide. Produces niaouli essential oil. May adapt to Kerala monsoon conditions but requires monitoring as can become invasive.
varieties.availability: Potentially available as ornamental, use with caution due to invasive potential

varieties.name: Standard Form
varieties.growing_notes: Dominant species in northern Australian melaleuca forests. High antibacterial activity in essential oil. May adapt to Kerala's monsoon rainfall patterns. Similar to M. cajuputi in tropical adaptation.

varieties.name: L. amboinense
varieties.growing_notes: Most likely Leptospermum species for Kerala. Extends from Queensland through Southeast Asian tropical regions. May tolerate Kerala's tropical monsoon climate better than temperate Leptospermum species.

varieties.name: L. javanicum
varieties.growing_notes: Southeast Asian endemic. Potential Kerala adaptation due to similar tropical climate. Rare.

varieties.name: L. parviflorum
varieties.growing_notes: Southeast Asian endemic. Possible Kerala cultivation but very limited information available.
